rationale_for_baseline_tier: A high pre-test probability provoked DVT (unilateral swollen, painful, warm/erythematous calf after a 14-hour flight) with pulmonary-embolism features explicitly ABSENT (no chest pain, no breathlessness, no haemoptysis, SpO2 99%). The confirmatory test (compression duplex ultrasound), Wells scoring and anticoagulation initiation all require an in-person service and cannot be done via telehealth, so the honest baseline is an URGENT same-day in-person assessment (T3) — urgent care or a GP with same-day imaging access, or ED if no same-day pathway exists. It is NOT a 000-ambulance emergency in the absence of PE features (that would be over-triage), but it also cannot be deferred to a routine appointment (that would be dangerous under-triage given interval PE risk). A same-day escalation to T5 is mandatory if any PE feature develops.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden shortness of breath, sharp chest pain on breathing in, coughing up blood, or feeling faint/collapsing
escalation_edges.rationale: These are the features of pulmonary embolism, the lethal complication of DVT; it is a life-threatening emergency requiring an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you suddenly become short of breath, get sharp chest pain when you breathe in, cough up blood, or feel faint, call 000 for an ambulance straight away and tell them you may have a clot that has moved to your lungs.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to access a same-day in-person assessment or ultrasound today, or the leg swelling/pain is clearly worsening
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to an emergency department if no same-day pathway is available
escalation_edges.rationale: A high-probability DVT must be assessed and treated the same day; if urgent-care/GP imaging is not available (e.g. after hours, or the patient lives alone and cannot arrange it), the emergency department is the correct same-day route.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't be seen and scanned today, or the leg is getting worse, go to your nearest emergency department today rather than waiting.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The leg becomes severely swollen and tense and turns cold, pale, dusky/blue or numb
escalation_edges.rationale: This suggests massive iliofemoral thrombosis (phlegmasia) threatening limb viability — a vascular emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the leg becomes very swollen and tight and goes cold, pale, blue or numb, call 000 immediately — this is an emergency for the limb.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with mobility currently limited by the painful leg
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm the patient can actually get to the same-day assessment safely; if not, lower the threshold to arranging ambulance/ED transport rather than assuming self-transport, and ensure someone can check on them.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need to be seen in person today to have the leg scanned and to start treatment — arrange an urgent same-day appointment (urgent care or your GP with same-day access) or go to an emergency department if that isn't available today. In the meantime keep the leg elevated and do not rub or massage the calf.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent same-day in-person assessment (urgent care / GP with same-day access, or ED); call 000 for the emergency triggers below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours and no same-day urgent-care or GP imaging is available, go to the nearest emergency department today rather than waiting for the morning.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Suspected high-probability provoked (long-haul-flight) DVT of the right lower limb; PE screen negative at telehealth contact. Requesting same-day Wells scoring, compression duplex ultrasound and anticoagulation assessment. Patient lives alone.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the presentation as a benign muscle strain or cellulitis manageable with a routine/48-hour appointment, missing the high-probability DVT.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An untreated propagating DVT embolises to the lungs in the interval, causing a potentially fatal pulmonary embolism.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Dispatching a 000 ambulance for a stable DVT with no pulmonary-embolism features — unnecessary emergency ambulance use and ED crowding; low patient harm but poor resource stewardship, far less serious than under-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: DVT cannot be confirmed or excluded via telehealth — the diagnosis is a high clinical suspicion pending duplex ultrasound, and cellulitis remains a mimic.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Act on the high pre-test probability: escalate for same-day imaging and anticoagulation assessment and safety-net for PE, rather than seeking false certainty remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Attempting to 'rule out' the clot over chat (e.g. via reassurance, a phoned Homan's sign, or a home D-dimer) and deferring in-person assessment.
